title_en: The Preservation of the Quran Versus the Bible
content_id:
Salah satu argumen terkuat yang dibuat Ibn Taymiyyah dalam Al-Jawab al-Sahih berkaitan dengan sejarah transmisi yang sangat berbeda antara Al-Quran dan teks-teks Alkitab. Al-Quran diwahyukan kepada Muhammad selama dua puluh tiga tahun, dihafal oleh komunitas sahabat yang besar yang sangat bangga dengan hafalan yang tepat dan lengkap, dituliskan selama masa hidup Nabi di bawah pengawasan langsungnya, dan dikompilasi menjadi satu kodeks otoritatif di bawah Khalifah Utsman dalam sekitar dua dekade setelah wafatnya Nabi. Sejak saat itu, Al-Quran telah dihafal secara keseluruhan oleh jutaan orang di setiap generasi, menggunakan sistem transmisi lisan yang terformalisasi dengan rantai perawi yang mapan (isnad al-qira'at) yang kembali kepada Nabi. Sistem ini memastikan bahwa setiap penyimpangan dari teks yang diterima dapat segera terdeteksi dan dikoreksi dengan dibandingkan dengan korpus yang dihafal.

Sejarah tekstual kitab-kitab Alkitab pada dasarnya berbeda. Kitab-kitab Perjanjian Lama disusun selama berabad-abad oleh banyak pengarang, diedit dan dikompilasi oleh sekolah-sekolah penyalin, dan ditransmisikan melalui salinan-salinan tulisan tangan yang mau tidak mau terakumulasi kesalahan-kesalahan penyalin dan perubahan-perubahan yang disengaja secara sesekali. Kitab-kitab Perjanjian Baru ditulis oleh berbagai pengarang dalam dekade-dekade setelah Yesus, disalin dengan tangan melalui banyak generasi, dan dipilih ke dalam koleksi kanonik melalui proses yang memakan waktu beberapa abad dan mengecualikan banyak teks-teks yang bersaing. Disiplin kritik tekstual, yang dikembangkan oleh para sarjana Kristen sendiri, telah mengidentifikasi ribuan varian di antara manuskrip-manuskrip yang masih ada dan terus memperdebatkan bacaan mana dari bagian-bagian yang diperdebatkan yang paling mewakili teks asli. Terjemahan-terjemahan Alkitab modern besar menyertakan catatan kaki yang mengakui varian-varian tekstual yang signifikan, mencerminkan percakapan keilmuan yang berlangsung tentang kata-kata aslinya.

Ibn Taymiyyah tidak mengklaim bahwa teks-teks Alkitab sepenuhnya rusak atau bahwa teks-teks tersebut tidak mengandung materi autentik dari wahyu-wahyu asli. Klaimnya lebih spesifik: transmisi teks-teks Alkitab tidak memberikan tingkat kepastian yang sama seperti yang diberikan oleh transmisi Al-Quran. Ketika seorang Muslim mengutip Al-Quran, mereka mengutip teks yang kata-katanya telah terus-menerus diverifikasi terhadap tradisi lisan yang hidup oleh ratusan juta penghafal selama empat belas abad. Ketika seorang Kristen mengutip Alkitab, mereka mengutip teks yang kata-kata persisnya direkonstruksi oleh para sarjana modern dari perbandingan manuskrip-manuskrip yang semuanya berasal setelah komposisi aslinya dalam periode waktu yang bervariasi. Ini bukan situasi epistemik yang setara, dan perbedaannya relevan ketika mengevaluasi otoritas kedua kitab suci tersebut.

Argumen ini sangat efektif dalam perdebatan dengan misionaris Kristen yang menggunakan otoritas Alkitab melawan klaim-klaim Islam, karena ia mengalihkan pertanyaan dari 'apa yang dikatakan teks' menjadi 'seberapa yakin kita tentang apa yang awalnya dikatakan teks.' Poin Ibn Taymiyyah bukan bahwa Al-Quran benar karena ia terpelihara dengan baik, melainkan bahwa pemeliharaan Al-Quran menjadikannya dasar yang lebih andal untuk argumen teologis daripada teks-teks yang transmisinya melibatkan jenis variasi dan intervensi manusiawi yang dokumentasikan oleh keilmuan Alkitab itu sendiri. Kontras dalam metodologi transmisi mencerminkan keyakinan teologis Islam yang lebih fundamental: bahwa Allah berjanji untuk memelihara wahyu terakhir (15:9), dan bahwa janji tersebut menemukan ekspresi konkretnya dalam tradisi hafalan dan bacaan Al-Quran yang tak terputus yang membentang dari komunitas Nabi hingga setiap masjid dan sekolah di dunia Muslim hari ini.
content_en:
One of the strongest arguments Ibn Taymiyyah makes in al-Jawab al-Sahih concerns the radically different transmission histories of the Quran and the Biblical texts. The Quran was revealed to Muhammad over a period of twenty-three years, memorized by a large community of Companions who took particular pride in precise and complete memorization, written down during the Prophet's lifetime under his direct supervision, and compiled into a single authoritative codex under the Caliph Uthman within roughly two decades of the Prophet's death. Since then, the Quran has been memorized in its entirety by millions of people in every generation, using a formalized system of oral transmission with established chains of reciters (isnad al-qira'at) going back to the Prophet. This system ensures that any deviation from the received text can be immediately detected and corrected by comparison with the memorized corpus.

The textual history of the Biblical books is fundamentally different. The Old Testament books were composed over many centuries by multiple authors, edited and compiled by scribal schools, and transmitted through handwritten copies that inevitably accumulated scribal errors and occasional deliberate changes. The New Testament books were written by various authors in the decades following Jesus, copied by hand through many generations, and selected into a canonical collection through a process that took several centuries and excluded many competing texts. The discipline of textual criticism, developed by Christian scholars themselves, has identified thousands of variants among surviving manuscripts and continues to debate which reading of disputed passages best represents the original text. Major modern Bible translations include footnotes acknowledging significant textual variants, reflecting the ongoing scholarly conversation about the original wording.

Ibn Taymiyyah does not claim that the Biblical texts are entirely corrupted or that they contain no authentic material from the original revelations. His claim is more specific: the transmission of the Biblical texts does not provide the same level of assurance that the transmission of the Quran provides. When a Muslim cites the Quran, they are citing a text whose wording has been continuously verified against a living oral tradition by hundreds of millions of memorizers across fourteen centuries. When a Christian cites the Bible, they are citing a text whose exact wording is reconstructed by modern scholars from a comparison of manuscripts that all postdate the original composition by varying periods of time. These are not equivalent epistemic situations, and the difference is relevant when evaluating the authority of the two scriptures.

This argument is particularly effective in debates with Christian missionaries who invoke Biblical authority against Islamic claims, because it shifts the question from 'what does the text say' to 'how confident can we be about what the text originally said.' Ibn Taymiyyah's point is not that the Quran is true because it is well-preserved, but rather that the Quran's preservation makes it a more reliable basis for theological argument than texts whose transmission involved the kinds of variation and human intervention that Biblical scholarship itself documents. The contrast in transmission methodology reflects a more fundamental Islamic theological conviction: that Allah promised to preserve the final revelation (15:9), and that promise finds its concrete expression in the unbroken tradition of Quran memorization and recitation that extends from the Prophet's community to every mosque and school in the Muslim world today.

One of the strongest arguments Ibn Taymiyyah makes in al-Jawab al-Sahih concerns the radically different transmission histories of the Quran and the Biblical texts. The Quran was revealed to Muhammad over a period of twenty-three years, memorized by a large community of Companions who took particular pride in precise and complete memorization, written down during النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's lifetime under his direct supervision, and compiled into a single authoritative codex under the Caliph Uthman within roughly two decades of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's death. Since then, the Quran has been memorized in its entirety by millions of people in every generation, using a formalized system of oral transmission with established chains of reciters (isnad al-qira'at) going back to النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم. This system ensures that any deviation from the received text can be immediately detected and corrected by comparison with the memorized corpus.

The Old Testament books were composed over many centuries by multiple authors, edited and compiled by scribal schools, and transmitted through handwritten copies that inevitably accumulated scribal errors and occasional deliberate changes. The discipline of textual criticism, developed by Christian scholars themselves, has identified thousands of variants among surviving manuscripts and continues to debate which reading of disputed passages best represents the original text. Major modern Bible translations include footnotes acknowledging significant textual variants, reflecting the ongoing scholarly conversation about the original wording.

Ibn Taymiyyah does not claim that the Biblical texts are entirely corrupted or that they contain no authentic material from the original revelations. His claim is more specific: the transmission of the Biblical texts does not provide the same level of assurance that the transmission of the Quran provides.
